news_summary: Today's human rights news highlights global unity against threats, the empowerment of youth as agents of change in the Balkans, inclusive recycling initiatives in Latin America, and the appointment of a young Spanish ambassador promoting positive social impact.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité des droits de l'homme d'aujourd'hui met en avant la solidarité mondiale face aux menaces, l'autonomisation des jeunes en tant qu'acteurs du changement dans les Balkans, des initiatives de recyclage inclusives en Amérique latine, ainsi que la nomination d'un jeune ambassadeur espagnol qui s'engage en faveur d'un impact social positif.
news_summary_es: Las noticias de hoy sobre derechos humanos destacan la unidad mundial frente a las amenazas, el empoderamiento de los jóvenes como agentes de cambio en los Balcanes, las iniciativas de reciclaje inclusivas en América Latina y el nombramiento de un joven embajador español que promueve un impacto social positivo.

articles.title: ‘Human rights are part of our DNA’: UN launches global alliance to counter rising threats
articles.summary: The UN human rights office (OHCHR) has launched a Global Alliance for Human Rights, a broad coalition aimed at placing the issue at the heart of decision-making, when conflict levels have reached a record high amid deepening inequality and accelerating climate change.
articles.content:
UN High Commissioner for Human Rights Volker Türk unveiled the initiative in Geneva, describing it as a direct response to what he called a world "in disarray." Speaking to UN News, Mr. Türk said the Alliance was designed to channel the energy of people who want change.

Tweet URL

A better world

"The vast majority of people around the world want a better world – they want a fairer world, a more just world," he said. "Human rights are part of who they are. They are part of our DNA."

The alliance aims to unite a sweeping range of actors – governments, civil society, businesses, cities, faith leaders, artists, academics and young people – around a shared framework built on three principles: imagine, dialogue, act.

The concept note published by OHCHR describes the human rights system as facing "unprecedented strain," with violations going unpunished, civic space shrinking, some States retreating from multilateral commitments and severe underfunding threatening the broader ecosystem.

Taking action

A series of concrete initiatives are set to launch this month, including a Global Helpdesk on Business and Human Rights, a RightsX Summit on digital innovation, and a Human Rights in Every Classroom programme to embed rights education at all levels of schooling.

Mr. Türk also set an ambitious target of expanding the network of “human rights cities” from 104 to 1,000 worldwide.

On the key issue of redress and accountability, Mr. Türk was direct. “One of the most important things is to give victims of human rights violations, of conflict, a voice,” he said, citing civilians affected by wars in Ukraine, Gaza, Lebanon, Myanmar, Sudan and Haiti.

Powerful responsibilities

He also addressed the growing power of large technology companies, warning that human rights must serve as both a guide and a regulatory framework when enormous financial and political power is being exercised.

The Alliance is structured as a long-term endeavour, not a one-off campaign, with a three-year milestone timed to the 80th anniversary of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) on 10 December 2028.

An annual Global Alliance Human Rights Forum, convened each year on that date, will track progress and set priorities.

Geneva was chosen as the natural home of the initiative, Mr. Türk said, given its concentration of human rights institutions – though he stressed the alliance would have “a home everywhere, wherever human rights are being discussed.”

Listen to our full interview with High Commissioner Volker Türk:
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the launch of a significant global alliance by the UN to promote and protect human rights amid rising global challenges. The initiative involves a broad coalition of actors and concrete programs aimed at embedding human rights in decision-making, education, and accountability, which has meaningful and broad societal impact.
articles.personality_title: UN launches Global Alliance to strengthen human rights worldwide
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The world is facing many challenges like more conflicts, growing inequality, and climate change. Human rights, which protect people's basic freedoms and dignity, are under pressure as violations increase and fewer governments cooperate to defend them.

**What happened** – The United Nations Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights (OHCHR) started a new Global Alliance for Human Rights. Announced by High Commissioner Volker Türk in Geneva, this alliance brings together governments, businesses, artists, young people, and others to work together on human rights. It focuses on imagining solutions, talking openly, and taking action.

**Impact** – This alliance is important because it connects many different groups to protect human rights better than before. It plans new projects like a helpdesk for business-related rights issues, a digital innovation summit, and teaching human rights in schools. It also wants to grow the number of “human rights cities” from 104 to 1,000, showing more communities standing up for rights.

**What's next step** – The alliance will continue for years, with a major check-in planned for December 10, 2028, the 80th anniversary of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights. Each year, they will hold a forum to review progress and decide what to focus on next. The alliance will be active everywhere human rights are discussed, not just in Geneva.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN’s new Global Alliance unites many voices worldwide to protect human rights through education, action, and cooperation over the coming years.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU lance une alliance mondiale pour renforcer les droits humains
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le monde fait face à de nombreux défis comme l’augmentation des conflits, les inégalités croissantes et le changement climatique. Les droits humains, qui protègent les libertés et la dignité des personnes, sont mis à rude épreuve avec plus de violations et moins de coopération entre États.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le Bureau du Haut-Commissariat des Nations Unies aux droits de l’homme (HCDH) a lancé une nouvelle Alliance mondiale pour les droits humains. Annoncée par le Haut-Commissaire Volker Türk à Genève, cette alliance réunit gouvernements, entreprises, artistes, jeunes et d’autres acteurs pour travailler ensemble sur les droits humains. Elle s’appuie sur l’imagination, le dialogue et l’action.

**Impact** – Cette alliance est importante car elle rassemble de nombreux groupes pour mieux protéger les droits humains. Elle prévoit des projets concrets comme un service d’aide sur les droits liés aux entreprises, un sommet sur l’innovation numérique, et l’enseignement des droits humains dans les écoles. Elle souhaite aussi faire passer le nombre de « villes des droits humains » de 104 à 1 000.

**Prochaine étape** – L’alliance fonctionnera sur plusieurs années, avec un bilan important prévu le 10 décembre 2028, 80e anniversaire de la Déclaration universelle des droits de l’homme. Chaque année, un forum évaluera les progrès et définira les priorités. L’alliance sera active partout où les droits humains sont discutés, pas seulement à Genève.

**Une phrase clé** – La nouvelle Alliance mondiale de l’ONU unit de nombreuses voix pour protéger les droits humains grâce à l’éducation, l’action et la coopération dans les années à venir.
articles.personality_title_es: ONU lanza alianza global para fortalecer los derechos humanos en todo el mundo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El mundo enfrenta muchos desafíos como más conflictos, desigualdad creciente y cambio climático. Los derechos humanos, que protegen las libertades básicas y la dignidad de las personas, están bajo presión con más violaciones y menos cooperación de los gobiernos.

**Qué pasó** – La Oficina del Alto Comisionado de las Naciones Unidas para los Derechos Humanos (ACNUDH) lanzó una nueva Alianza Global por los Derechos Humanos. Anunciada por el Alto Comisionado Volker Türk en Ginebra, esta alianza reúne a gobiernos, empresas, artistas, jóvenes y otros para trabajar juntos en los derechos humanos. Se basa en imaginar soluciones, dialogar y actuar.

**Impacto** – Esta alianza es importante porque conecta a muchos grupos para proteger mejor los derechos humanos. Planea nuevos proyectos como un centro de ayuda para temas de derechos en negocios, una cumbre sobre innovación digital y enseñar derechos humanos en las escuelas. También quiere aumentar las “ciudades de derechos humanos” de 104 a 1,000.

**Próximo paso** – La alianza continuará por varios años, con una revisión importante el 10 de diciembre de 2028, aniversario 80 de la Declaración Universal de Derechos Humanos. Cada año habrá un foro para revisar avances y definir prioridades. La alianza estará activa donde se discutan derechos humanos, no solo en Ginebra.

**Frase clave** – La nueva Alianza Global de la ONU une muchas voces para proteger los derechos humanos mediante la educación, la acción y la cooperación en los próximos años.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a diverse circle of glowing, interlinked DNA strands forming a globe at the center, surrounded by symbolic silhouettes of people holding hands and abstract representations of cities, books, and digital networks, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones to evoke unity, human rights, and global collaboration.

articles.title: Spain prodigy Lamine Yamal named UN Goodwill Ambassador on World Cup opening day
articles.summary: The biggest-ever football World Cup begins on Thursday, across three countries and two continents, with over 100 games. The UN is raising awareness of the game’s powerful capacity to serve as a platform for sustainable development and social justice.
articles.content:
The supremely talented Spanish team is one of the main favourites to win the event and its teenage superstar, Lamine Yamal, promises to be one of the tournament’s standout performers.

On Thursday, ahead of the first game between Mexico and South Africa, The UN children’s agency (UNICEF) announced that Yamal has been made its youngest-ever Goodwill Ambassador, focusing on children’s right to play and supporting those living in humanitarian emergencies, such as conflict or natural disasters.

Children’s rights are a cause close to the Barcelona midfielder’s heart. On World Children's Day in 2024, as part of a UNICEF campaign, he wrote a heartfelt letter to his younger brother sharing his hopes for children everywhere.

Last year, he handed his social media accounts to a nine-year-old girl from Equatorial Guinea – his mother's home country – allowing her to share with the world her daily life experiences, far from the glamour of Barcelona’s Camp Nou stadium.

Yamal’s appointment was announced on the International Day of Play, marked annually on 11 June. UNICEF has revealed that more than 90 million children under the age of five do not have any playthings such as store-bought toys, household objects or other simple resources for play at home, while 80 million children aged two to four do not play with a parent or caregiver at home.

Scoring (sustainable development) goals

On the eve of the event, the UN released a study showing how football can help to change lives for the better and drive progress towards a fairer world.

The report examines the growing impact of the Football for the Goals initiative, which brings together clubs, associations, businesses and civil society to support sustainable development.

The UN initiative encourages organisations in the football world to align their activities with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), a blueprint to end poverty, reduce inequality and build a better future for all by 2030.

Reaching those most in need

Data gathered from participating organisations show that football-based projects are making a difference in disadvantaged communities.

Many initiatives focus on reducing inequality, improving access to education and healthcare and supporting partnerships that bring people together. Examples include creating safe spaces for young people, empowering women and girls and supporting community wellbeing.

By tapping into football’s popularity, organisations are able to engage people who might otherwise be difficult to reach, turning enthusiasm for the sport into positive action.

With just a few years left to achieve the SDGs, the report underlines the need for innovative approaches that deliver results on the ground. Football, it suggests, offers a unique opportunity to unite people and drive progress in ways that are both practical and inspiring.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the appointment of a young football prodigy as UNICEF Goodwill Ambassador focusing on children's rights and humanitarian support, linked to a broad UN initiative leveraging football to promote sustainable development and social justice globally. This represents a meaningful, inspiring real-world impact with significant scope and substance.
articles.personality_title: Young football star Lamine Yamal becomes UNICEF Goodwill Ambassador during World Cup
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The 2024 football World Cup is starting across three countries and two continents. It is the biggest tournament ever, with over 100 games. The United Nations (UN) is using the event to highlight how football can help promote fair treatment and better lives for people around the world.

**What happened** – On the day of the World Cup opening match, UNICEF, the UN’s children’s agency, named Spanish teenage football player Lamine Yamal as its youngest-ever Goodwill Ambassador. This means he will help raise awareness about children’s right to play and support those living through emergencies like wars or natural disasters. Yamal has shown his commitment to these causes by sharing messages for children and giving a young girl from Equatorial Guinea time on his social media to tell her story.

**Impact** – Yamal’s new role is important because it uses his fame to bring attention to children’s needs, especially those in tough situations. UNICEF points out that millions of young children around the world don’t have toys or people to play with. At the same time, the UN study shows football helps unite communities and supports projects that improve education, health, and equality. This makes football more than just a game—it becomes a tool to help solve real problems.

**What’s next step** – As the World Cup continues, Yamal will take part in campaigns and events to spread messages about children’s rights and play. Meanwhile, football clubs and organizations involved in the UN’s Football for the Goals initiative will keep working on projects that help people in need. The hope is to use football’s global popularity to meet important goals for a fairer world by 2030.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Teen football star Lamine Yamal’s appointment as UNICEF Goodwill Ambassador highlights how sport can support children’s rights and promote positive change worldwide during the World Cup.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le jeune prodige du football Lamine Yamal nommé ambassadeur de bonne volonté de l’UNICEF lors de la Coupe du Monde
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Coupe du Monde de football 2024 débute dans trois pays sur deux continents. C’est le plus grand tournoi jamais organisé, avec plus de 100 matchs. Les Nations Unies (ONU) utilisent cet événement pour montrer comment le football peut aider à promouvoir la justice sociale et de meilleures conditions de vie dans le monde.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le jour du match d’ouverture, l’UNICEF, l’agence pour les enfants de l’ONU, a nommé Lamine Yamal, jeune footballeur espagnol, plus jeune ambassadeur de bonne volonté de son histoire. Il va aider à sensibiliser sur le droit des enfants à jouer et soutenir ceux vivant dans des situations d’urgence comme les conflits ou les catastrophes naturelles. Yamal a déjà montré son engagement en partageant des messages pour les enfants et en donnant à une fillette de Guinée équatoriale du temps sur ses réseaux sociaux pour raconter son quotidien.

**Impact** – Ce rôle est important car il utilise la popularité de Yamal pour attirer l’attention sur les besoins des enfants, surtout ceux en difficulté. L’UNICEF indique que des millions de jeunes enfants dans le monde n’ont pas de jouets ni de proches pour jouer avec eux. En même temps, une étude de l’ONU montre que le football aide à rassembler les communautés et soutient des projets améliorant l’éducation, la santé et l’égalité. Le football devient ainsi un outil pour résoudre des problèmes concrets.

**Prochaines étapes** – Pendant la Coupe du Monde, Yamal participera à des campagnes pour promouvoir les droits des enfants et l’importance du jeu. Parallèlement, les clubs et organisations impliqués dans l’initiative Football for the Goals continueront à développer des projets aidant les personnes dans le besoin. L’objectif est d’utiliser la popularité mondiale du football pour atteindre des buts importants d’ici 2030.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La nomination du jeune footballeur Lamine Yamal comme ambassadeur de bonne volonté de l’UNICEF montre comment le sport peut soutenir les droits des enfants et encourager des changements positifs dans le monde pendant la Coupe du Monde.
articles.personality_title_es: El joven prodigio del fútbol Lamine Yamal nombrado Embajador de Buena Voluntad de UNICEF en el Mundial
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Copa Mundial de fútbol 2024 comienza en tres países y dos continentes. Es el torneo más grande de la historia, con más de 100 partidos. Las Naciones Unidas (ONU) están aprovechando el evento para mostrar cómo el fútbol puede ayudar a promover la justicia social y mejorar las vidas de las personas en todo el mundo.

**Qué pasó** – El día del partido inaugural, UNICEF, la agencia de la ONU para la infancia, nombró al joven futbolista español Lamine Yamal como su Embajador de Buena Voluntad más joven. Esto significa que ayudará a crear conciencia sobre el derecho de los niños a jugar y apoyará a quienes viven en emergencias como guerras o desastres naturales. Yamal ya ha demostrado su compromiso compartiendo mensajes para los niños y cediendo sus redes sociales a una niña de Guinea Ecuatorial para que cuente su historia.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo papel de Yamal es importante porque usa su fama para llamar la atención sobre las necesidades de los niños, especialmente los que están en situaciones difíciles. UNICEF señala que millones de niños pequeños en el mundo no tienen juguetes ni personas con quienes jugar. Al mismo tiempo, un estudio de la ONU muestra que el fútbol une a las comunidades y apoya proyectos que mejoran la educación, la salud y la igualdad. Así, el fútbol se convierte en una herramienta para ayudar a resolver problemas reales.

**Próximos pasos** – Durante el Mundial, Yamal participará en campañas para difundir mensajes sobre los derechos de los niños y la importancia del juego. Mientras tanto, los clubes y organizaciones que forman parte de la iniciativa Football for the Goals seguirán trabajando en proyectos para ayudar a quienes lo necesitan. La esperanza es usar la popularidad mundial del fútbol para alcanzar metas importantes hacia un mundo más justo para 2030.

**Frase clave** – El nombramiento del joven futbolista Lamine Yamal como Embajador de Buena Voluntad de UNICEF muestra cómo el deporte puede apoyar los derechos de los niños y promover cambios positivos durante el Mundial.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing young soccer ball gently cradled by two large, protective hands above a vibrant football pitch made of patchwork fields symbolizing diverse communities, with small playful children’s shadows dancing around the ball, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones and muted blues.

articles.title: Trash and dignity: The rise of inclusive recycling projects in Latin America
articles.summary: Across Latin America, initiatives like Eloísa Cartonera, Amazoniko, migrant recycling programs, and women-led cooperatives show how recycling can create jobs, dignity, and circular economy innovation.
articles.content:
This story is part of Global Voices’ May 2026 Spotlight series, “Global crisis, local solutions.” You can support this coverage by donating here.

Across Latin America, recycling plays a far more critical social and economic role than global averages might suggest. Despite worldwide advances in waste management, the region’s average recycling rate remains strikingly low — around four percent. Yet, within this limited formal system, an estimated two million people make a living through informal recycling, forming an essential but often invisible backbone of urban waste recovery.

For many of these workers, recycling is more than an environmental practice: it’s a means of survival. Women, migrants, displaced populations, and unhoused individuals living in precarious urban conditions are among those who depend on collecting, sorting, and selling recyclable materials to sustain their daily lives. In this context, waste becomes both a resource and a lifeline.

So, how do different initiatives across the region support these communities? And how can recycling-focused projects go beyond environmental benefits to foster dignity, generate income, and open pathways toward social inclusion for some of the most vulnerable people in Latin America’s cities?

Eloísa Cartonera: Books made by cartoneros in Argentina

Eloísa Cartonera is one of Latin America’s most distinctive independent publishing projects. Founded in Buenos Aires in 2002, during Argentina’s devastating economic crisis, the initiative emerged at a time of mass unemployment, social unrest, and growing poverty following the country’s 2001 collapse.

The project works with cartoneros (people who collect and sell discarded cardboard), many of whom are unhoused or living in precarious conditions. Eloísa Cartonera buys cardboard at fair prices and employs workers to handcraft and paint books, turning recycled materials into unique works of art. Profits from sales are shared among the workers and organizers.

Founded by writer Washington Cucurto, visual artist Javier Barilaro, and a group of artists and activists, the collective also promotes accessible literature by publishing both emerging and established Latin American authors, many of whom donate their rights to support the project.

Each book is individually painted with colorful tempera, making no two copies alike. More than two decades later, Eloísa Cartonera has inspired over 50 similar cartonera publishing houses around the world.

Amazoniko: From recyclers to urban environmental leaders in Colombia

Amazoniko was born in 2017 with an idea that goes far beyond recycling. For its founder, Daniel Rodríguez Paredes, the problem was never just the trash itself, but the loss of all the environmental, economic, and human value behind each piece of waste. “When a material ends up buried or improperly disposed of, not only is an object lost, but water, energy, human labor, and natural resources are also wasted,” he explained to Global Voices in a phone call. What began as a platform to facilitate recycling from homes ended up becoming a circular economy model that connects citizens, businesses, recyclers, and processors.

In Colombia, thousands of recyclers work in informal conditions, often without contracts, social security, or recognition. Faced with this reality, Amazoniko decided to put people at the center of the system. The project seeks to understand the needs of those who sustain the recycling chain every day: from households that require environmental education to recyclers who need better working conditions and economic stability. The platform organizes collection routes, improves access to quality materials, and promotes a different narrative around the recycler, who is no longer seen as marginalized but rather as an “environmental advisor” within the communities. In addition to improving income and stability, Amazoniko promotes incentives such as digital tips and microloans linked to the recyclers’ performance.

The human impact of the project is reflected in stories like that of Cristian, Amazoniko’s first recycler. He began by cycling around the city collecting recyclable materials and helped build the system through his daily experience. Today, he is the company’s operations director and one of the most important figures in the operation. For Daniel, his story demonstrates that social inclusion is not just about connecting vulnerable people to a project, but about recognizing their abilities, opening up real opportunities for growth, and dignifying a historically stigmatized job.

Venezuelan migrants recycle to survive

For many Venezuelan migrants in Colombia, recycling has become an important means of survival and economic integration. Colombia hosts one of the largest populations of Venezuelan refugees and migrants in the world, with more than three million having crossed the border in recent years in search of safety, employment, and access to basic services. Faced with barriers to formal employment, many migrants turn to informal work, including collecting and selling recyclable materials.

In Barranquilla’s southeastern neighborhoods, such as Brisas del Río, recycling provides a source of income for both Colombian and Venezuelan families living in vulnerable conditions. Since 2019, the United Nations Refugee Agency (UNHCR), together with Pastoral Social Cáritas Barranquilla and support from the Italian Agency for Development Cooperation (AICS), has implemented programs aimed at dignifying the work of environmental recyclers. The initiative has included training, protective equipment, and support services for recycling workers and their families.

Like many informal settlements across Colombia, Brisas del Río faces significant challenges, including inadequate access to water, sanitation, transportation, healthcare, and education. In this context, recycling functions not only as a waste-management activity, but also as a pathway to livelihood, community integration, and resilience for displaced populations.

Since 2022, 45 recyclers have received training and safety materials to help them carry out their work more effectively and with lower health risks. As one participant explained on the project’s website, “Recycling is our way of supporting ourselves now.” For many Venezuelan migrants, collecting recyclable materials offers a vital source of daily income in communities where poverty, informality, and limited access to public services remain widespread.

In Ecuador, 70 percent of recyclers are women

Recycling plays a crucial yet often undervalued and stigmatized role in society, despite being essential to the transition toward a circular economy. People working as street waste pickers — mostly women — form the first link in the recovery chain, collecting and sorting materials that contribute directly to urban cleanliness and resource reuse.

In Ecuador, around 20,000 people work as recyclers, 1,500 of them organized through the National Reclycers of Ecuador (Renarec), which groups under its umbrella more than 50 organizations dedicated to reducing waste, and protecting street workers and recyclers against precarity.



As told by UN Ecuador, The experience of Doña Blanca Lara, a recycler in Quito, illustrates this reality. After a life marked by hardship and responsibility for her family, she and her daughter rely entirely on recycling for their livelihood, earning small and variable incomes depending on the type of material collected. Despite their contribution to environmental sustainability, they face discrimination, unsafe working conditions, and limited social protection.

Across Ecuador, thousands of recyclers share similar experiences, with high levels of informality and gender inequality. Although their work is essential to waste management and environmental recovery, it remains largely invisible, highlighting the need for greater recognition, institutional support, and social awareness of their economic and environmental contribution.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights inclusive recycling initiatives across Latin America that create jobs, dignity, and social inclusion for vulnerable populations such as women, migrants, and displaced people. These projects provide meaningful economic opportunities, promote social recognition, and support environmental sustainability, impacting millions and addressing poverty and marginalization in urban areas. The detailed examples and broad regional scope demonstrate significant positive real-world impact and substance.
articles.personality_title: Inclusive recycling projects in Latin America create jobs and dignity
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In Latin America, recycling rates are low compared to the rest of the world, but many people rely on informal recycling to make a living. These workers often include women, migrants, and people facing tough living conditions. Recycling here is not just about the environment; it’s a way to survive and earn money.

**What happened** – Several projects across Latin America are helping these recyclers find better jobs and respect. For example, Eloísa Cartonera in Argentina hires people who collect cardboard to make colorful handmade books. Amazoniko in Colombia connects recyclers with businesses and communities, improving their income and working conditions. In Colombia, programs also support Venezuelan migrants by providing training and safety gear for recycling work. In Ecuador, most recyclers are women who face challenges but keep cities clean and help reuse materials.

**Impact** – These projects do more than recycle trash. They give people steady jobs, improve their health and safety, and help them feel valued in their communities. For instance, Amazoniko turned a recycler into a company leader, showing how recycling can lead to real opportunities. The programs also support vulnerable groups like migrants and women, helping them build a better future while protecting the environment.

**What's next step** – These initiatives inspire more inclusive recycling efforts across Latin America. The next steps include expanding training, improving social protections, and raising public awareness about the important role recyclers play. Supporting these workers can lead to stronger communities and better waste management in cities.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Recycling projects in Latin America are creating jobs and respect for vulnerable people while helping cities manage waste more sustainably.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des projets de recyclage inclusifs en Amérique latine créent emplois et dignité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En Amérique latine, le taux de recyclage est faible comparé au reste du monde, mais beaucoup de personnes vivent du recyclage informel. Ces travailleurs sont souvent des femmes, des migrants ou des personnes en situation difficile. Pour eux, recycler n’est pas seulement protéger l’environnement, c’est un moyen de survivre et de gagner de l’argent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Plusieurs projets à travers l’Amérique latine aident ces recycleurs à trouver de meilleurs emplois et du respect. Par exemple, Eloísa Cartonera en Argentine emploie des personnes qui collectent du carton pour fabriquer des livres faits main et colorés. Amazoniko en Colombie met en relation recycleurs, entreprises et habitants pour améliorer leurs revenus et conditions de travail. En Colombie, des programmes soutiennent aussi les migrants vénézuéliens avec des formations et du matériel de sécurité. En Équateur, la majorité des recycleurs sont des femmes qui, malgré les difficultés, contribuent à la propreté des villes et à la réutilisation des matériaux.

**Impact** – Ces projets vont au-delà du recyclage. Ils offrent des emplois stables, améliorent la santé et la sécurité, et valorisent ces personnes dans leurs communautés. Par exemple, Amazoniko a transformé un recycleur en directeur de l’entreprise, montrant que le recyclage peut ouvrir de vraies opportunités. Ces initiatives soutiennent aussi des groupes vulnérables comme les migrants et les femmes, les aidant à construire un avenir meilleur tout en protégeant l’environnement.

**Prochaines étapes** – Ces initiatives inspirent plus de projets de recyclage inclusifs en Amérique latine. Les prochaines étapes sont d’élargir les formations, d’améliorer la protection sociale et de sensibiliser le public au rôle important des recycleurs. Soutenir ces travailleurs peut renforcer les communautés et améliorer la gestion des déchets en ville.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les projets de recyclage en Amérique latine créent des emplois et du respect pour les personnes vulnérables tout en aidant les villes à mieux gérer leurs déchets.
articles.personality_title_es: Proyectos inclusivos de reciclaje en América Latina generan empleos y dignidad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En América Latina, las tasas de reciclaje son bajas en comparación con el resto del mundo, pero muchas personas dependen del reciclaje informal para vivir. Estos trabajadores suelen ser mujeres, migrantes y personas en condiciones difíciles. Para ellos, reciclar no es solo cuidar el medio ambiente, es una forma de sobrevivir y ganar dinero.

**Qué pasó** – Varios proyectos en América Latina están ayudando a estos recicladores a conseguir mejores trabajos y respeto. Por ejemplo, Eloísa Cartonera en Argentina emplea a personas que recolectan cartón para hacer libros artesanales y coloridos. Amazoniko en Colombia conecta a recicladores con empresas y comunidades para mejorar sus ingresos y condiciones laborales. En Colombia, programas apoyan a migrantes venezolanos con capacitación y equipos de seguridad. En Ecuador, la mayoría de recicladores son mujeres que, a pesar de las dificultades, mantienen limpias las ciudades y ayudan a reutilizar materiales.

**Impacto** – Estos proyectos hacen más que reciclar basura. Ofrecen empleos estables, mejoran la salud y seguridad, y dan valor a estas personas en sus comunidades. Por ejemplo, Amazoniko convirtió a un reciclador en director de la empresa, mostrando que el reciclaje puede abrir verdaderas oportunidades. También apoyan a grupos vulnerables como migrantes y mujeres, ayudándolos a construir un mejor futuro mientras protegen el medio ambiente.

**Próximo paso** – Estas iniciativas inspiran más proyectos de reciclaje inclusivo en América Latina. Los siguientes pasos incluyen ampliar la capacitación, mejorar la protección social y aumentar la conciencia pública sobre la importancia de los recicladores. Apoyar a estos trabajadores puede fortalecer las comunidades y mejorar la gestión de residuos en las ciudades.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los proyectos de reciclaje en América Latina crean empleos y respeto para personas vulnerables mientras ayudan a las ciudades a manejar sus residuos de forma más sostenible.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of colorful, hand-painted cardboard books stacked alongside a bicycle loaded with neatly sorted recyclable materials, set against a backdrop of a vibrant Latin American urban neighborhood with simple, earthy tones and symbolic silhouettes of diverse people working together in harmony.

articles.title: The youth of Croatia, Serbia, and Bosnia and Herzegovina as bearers of collective change
articles.summary: For young people of the region, cooperation is not a political slogan but a real experience gained through non-formal education, training, and civil society initiatives.
articles.content:
This article by Iris Knežević first appeared in Balkan Diskurs on March 10, 2026. An edited version is being republished on Global Voices under a content partnership agreement with the Post-Conflict Research Center (PCRC). While regional politicians are increasingly embracing nationalist rhetoric and deepening divisions, young people from Croatia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and Serbia are taking a different approach – one based on daily collaboration, solidarity, and learning.

For them, regional cooperation is not a political slogan but a real experience gained through non-formal education, training, and civil society initiatives.

Collaboration takes place outside the formal education system through workshops in Sarajevo, study trips in Zagreb, and joint projects in Belgrade. In these spaces, young people discuss responsibility, the culture of memory, and the legacy of the wars of the 1990s, developing critical thinking skills and asking questions that often remain unspoken in formal institutions.

Non-formal education: A space of freedom and solidarity

Unlike formal education, which is often burdened by nationalist narratives and selective interpretations of the past, informal education provides space for critical reflection and open dialogue. Regional training, youth exchanges, and civil society workshops help young people understand the complexity of the past and the importance of cooperation in the present.

Vid Radičević is a 22-year-old from Zemun, Serbia. For him, regional cooperation is an opportunity to encounter new people, attitudes, places, and customs, and to “understand everyone without any need for translation – however these languages are classified in books.” He continued, “It’s important to understand and get to know realities other than our own so that we can better understand where we are, what we would like our environment to look like, and what we can do in our own community to make it more open, or more focused on what we want to learn from it.”

Civil society organizations have been bringing young people together around the topics of transitional justice, human rights, and regional solidarity for almost three decades. Study trips, visits to places of suffering, and public discussions show that facing the past is not betrayal of one’s own country, but an act of social responsibility.

At the training sessions, young people often hear the perspectives of those “from the other side” for the first time. This is usually their chance to talk without dominating political rhetoric and many are struck by how similar their experiences are, given the exclusive political environment they face in Zagreb, Sarajevo, and Belgrade.

Regional cooperation as empowerment

Amid the frequent political tensions among Croatia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and Serbia, regional youth cooperation acts as a form of civil resistance. Young people refuse to accept the logic of collective guilt and permanent intolerance, choosing solidarity instead.

Enis Mlivić, a 24-year-old from Breza, Bosnia and Herzegovina, said that regional cooperation is invigorating because it breaks through the feeling of isolation. He explained, “When you know that there are people in Belgrade, Zagreb, Podgorica, or Pristina who are fighting for similar values in the field of human rights, responsibility, and cultures of remembrance, you realize that you aren’t alone. This gives legitimacy and strength. It connects resources, knowledge, and experiences, but more importantly, it connects people.”

The solidarity of young people in the region is evident in joint campaigns against hate speech, support for activists and protests, performances, and other activities, as well as in simple gestures like attending commemorations in neighboring countries.

Dealing with the past together

Without building space for dialogue and mutual understanding, regional cooperation is unsustainable — that’s the lesson young activists are learning. The denial of war crimes, relativization of judicial verdicts, and political instrumentalization of war trauma deepen mistrust and close the space for dialogue.

That is why regional initiatives that connect young people are extremely important, creating networks of trust that transcend borders and day-to-day politics. When young people analyze the judgments of international courts together, visit places of suffering, or work on joint research, they build a culture of responsibility that is a prerequisite for lasting peace.

Tena Vizinger, a 21-year-old from Zagreb, believes that regional cooperation in dealing with the past is crucial for the future. She clarifies that she is mainly referring to European Union accession and overcoming mutual barriers in this process: “I think that dealing with the past together is possible, and that it can be facilitated through structural changes — for example, in education. Croatia and other countries have really high-quality and competent teachers who promote the idea of ​​mutual dialogue and I hope that, in the long term, this will result in concrete cooperation between countries in dealing with the past.”

Despite challenges ranging from limited civil society resources to political pressure, regional youth activism continues to grow. Young people work together in trainings, workshops, and joint projects, showing that initiative and solidarity can overcome institutional obstacles. Although they did not choose the wars of the 1990s, they are aware that the legacy of conflict continues to shape social relations.

Through these initiatives, young people show that cooperation is not just an ideal but a practical reality. Joint training, visits to places of suffering, such as former camp sites, memorials and other documented sites of wartime violence, and civil society projects help to overcome stereotypes, open space for dialogue, and lay the foundations for concrete collective action, suggesting that the future of the region can be defined not by divisions, but by collaboration and joint effort.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a meaningful and inspiring real-world effort by youth from Croatia, Serbia, and Bosnia and Herzegovina to overcome nationalist divisions through non-formal education, regional cooperation, and civil society initiatives. This fosters dialogue, solidarity, and a culture of responsibility around difficult historical legacies, contributing to lasting peace and reconciliation in a historically conflict-affected region. The story has broad societal significance, clear substance, and focuses on a single impactful topic.
articles.personality_title: Young people in the Balkans build peace through cooperation and education
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the Balkans, countries like Croatia, Serbia, and Bosnia and Herzegovina have a history marked by conflict during the 1990s. Political leaders often use nationalist ideas that create division. However, many young people in these countries are choosing a different path.

**What happened** – Young people from these three countries are working together through non-formal education programs, workshops, and civil society projects. They meet in cities like Sarajevo, Zagreb, and Belgrade to learn about history, human rights, and the importance of cooperation. These activities help them discuss difficult topics like war memories and justice in a way that schools often do not.

**Impact** – This cooperation helps young people see beyond political divisions. They share experiences, support each other, and build trust across borders. For example, young people visit places affected by war and hear different perspectives, which helps them understand each other better. This teamwork creates a strong network that encourages peace and challenges hatred.

**What's next step** – These youth initiatives are expected to keep growing, despite political challenges. They may influence changes in education systems and promote more open dialogue in the future. By working together, young people hope to make lasting improvements in how their societies deal with the past and prepare for joining the European Union.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Young people in Croatia, Serbia, and Bosnia and Herzegovina are overcoming past conflicts by building trust and cooperation through education and shared projects.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les jeunes des Balkans construisent la paix grâce à la coopération et à l’éducation
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans les Balkans, des pays comme la Croatie, la Serbie et la Bosnie-Herzégovine ont une histoire marquée par des conflits dans les années 1990. Les dirigeants politiques utilisent souvent des idées nationalistes qui divisent. Pourtant, beaucoup de jeunes de ces pays choisissent une autre voie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des jeunes de ces trois pays travaillent ensemble à travers des programmes d’éducation non formelle, des ateliers et des projets de la société civile. Ils se réunissent dans des villes comme Sarajevo, Zagreb et Belgrade pour apprendre l’histoire, les droits humains et l’importance de la coopération. Ces activités leur permettent de discuter de sujets difficiles comme la mémoire de la guerre et la justice, ce que les écoles ne font pas toujours.

**Impact** – Cette coopération aide les jeunes à dépasser les divisions politiques. Ils partagent leurs expériences, se soutiennent et construisent la confiance au-delà des frontières. Par exemple, ils visitent des lieux marqués par la guerre et entendent différents points de vue, ce qui améliore leur compréhension mutuelle. Ce travail en commun crée un réseau solide qui encourage la paix et combat la haine.

**Prochaine étape** – Ces initiatives de jeunesse devraient continuer à se développer malgré les défis politiques. Elles pourraient influencer des changements dans les systèmes éducatifs et promouvoir un dialogue plus ouvert à l’avenir. En travaillant ensemble, les jeunes espèrent améliorer durablement la manière dont leurs sociétés gèrent le passé et préparent leur entrée dans l’Union européenne.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les jeunes de Croatie, Serbie et Bosnie-Herzégovine surmontent les conflits passés en construisant confiance et coopération grâce à l’éducation et à des projets communs.
articles.personality_title_es: Los jóvenes de los Balcanes construyen la paz mediante la cooperación y la educación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En los Balcanes, países como Croacia, Serbia y Bosnia y Herzegovina tienen una historia marcada por conflictos en la década de 1990. Los líderes políticos a menudo usan ideas nacionalistas que crean divisiones. Sin embargo, muchos jóvenes de estos países están eligiendo un camino diferente.

**Qué pasó** – Jóvenes de estos tres países trabajan juntos a través de programas de educación no formal, talleres y proyectos de la sociedad civil. Se reúnen en ciudades como Sarajevo, Zagreb y Belgrado para aprender sobre historia, derechos humanos y la importancia de la cooperación. Estas actividades les ayudan a hablar sobre temas difíciles como la memoria de la guerra y la justicia, algo que las escuelas a menudo no abordan.

**Impacto** – Esta cooperación ayuda a los jóvenes a ver más allá de las divisiones políticas. Comparten experiencias, se apoyan y construyen confianza entre fronteras. Por ejemplo, visitan lugares afectados por la guerra y escuchan diferentes puntos de vista, lo que mejora su comprensión mutua. Este trabajo en equipo crea una red fuerte que fomenta la paz y desafía el odio.

**Próximo paso** – Se espera que estas iniciativas juveniles sigan creciendo a pesar de los desafíos políticos. Podrían influir en cambios en los sistemas educativos y promover un diálogo más abierto en el futuro. Trabajando juntos, los jóvenes esperan mejorar cómo sus sociedades enfrentan el pasado y se preparan para unirse a la Unión Europea.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los jóvenes de Croacia, Serbia y Bosnia y Herzegovina superan los conflictos pasados construyendo confianza y cooperación a través de la educación y proyectos conjuntos.
articles.image_prompt: Three intertwined, gently glowing hands made of layered paper maps of Croatia, Serbia, and Bosnia and Herzegovina, connected by delicate threads of light weaving through symbolic landmarks and shared cultural motifs, set against a softly colored backdrop of overlapping city skylines and peaceful gathering spaces, rendered in warm, natural tones with a hand-drawn, painterly style.
